title: Top 10 Stage-to-Screen Movies and ‘One Night in Miami’
format: News on a week of new movie announcements, a review of Regina King's One Night in Miami, a split top 10 of stage-to-screen adaptations (five plays, five musicals), and Sean's interview with star Kingsley Ben-Adir.

segments.heading: Movie News Roundup
segments.note: fast-formed opinions on a week of announcements

films.title: The Whale
films.note: Darren Aronofsky's adaptation of Samuel D. Hunter's play, with Brendan Fraser as a reclusive, severely obese English teacher trying to reconnect with his daughter.

films.title: Men
films.note: Alex Garland writing and directing Jessie Buckley as a woman on a solo countryside vacation after her ex-husband's death.

films.title: White Noise
films.note: Noah Baumbach adapting Don DeLillo's supposedly unadaptable novel, with Adam Driver and Greta Gerwig (her first film in six years).

films.title: The Tragedy of Macbeth
films.note: Joel Coen's black-and-white Shakespeare, shot with Denzel Washington and Frances McDormand, for A24.

films.title: One Night in Miami
films.note: Regina King's directorial debut, written by Kemp Powers, imagines Malcolm X, Muhammad Ali, Jim Brown, and Sam Cooke spending one long night together in a Miami motel room after Ali's title win over Sonny Liston in February 1964. Kingsley Ben-Adir plays Malcolm X, Eli Goree plays Ali, Aldis Hodge plays Jim Brown, and Leslie Odom Jr. plays Sam Cooke.

segments.heading: Top 10 Stage-to-Screen: The Plays
segments.note: five picks each, one Shakespeare apiece, Broadway/stage plays only

picks.title: Henry V
picks.note: Laurence Olivier's Technicolor Shakespeare, with production design Sean compares to Wes Anderson and one of the great St. Crispin's Day speeches on film.

picks.title: Romeo + Juliet
picks.note: Baz Luhrmann keeps every line of Shakespeare's dialogue while staging it as a teen spectacle; a formative movie for Amanda's generation, with Leonardo DiCaprio and Claire Danes.

picks.title: Brief Encounter
picks.note: David Lean directing Noel Coward's one-act play about an illicit almost-affair that plays out mostly at a train station.

picks.title: The Philadelphia Story
picks.note: One of two Katharine Hepburn picks: the play-and-film combo that reversed her 'box office poison' run after Bringing Up Baby.

picks.title: The Lion in Winter
picks.note: James Goldman's screenplay from his own play, with Hepburn and Peter O'Toole as Eleanor of Aquitaine and Henry II tearing into each other over Christmas.

picks.title: Sleuth
picks.note: Joseph L. Mankiewicz's last film, from Anthony Shaffer's play, with Michael Caine and Laurence Olivier trading cat-and-mouse dialogue for two and a half hours. Remade in 2007 with Jude Law; not to be confused with the similar Deathtrap.

picks.title: Dial M for Murder
picks.note: Hitchcock's stage-play adaptation, where the confined geography of one room becomes the whole tension.

picks.title: Amadeus
picks.note: Milos Forman's Mozart-and-Salieri epic with Tom Hulce and F. Murray Abraham; Amanda says it would have made her list too.

picks.title: Secret Honor
picks.note: A bonus rewatch, not an official pick: Robert Altman's one-set Nixon monologue, with Philip Baker Hall memorizing what amounts to a 200-page speech. A favorite of Paul Thomas Anderson's.

picks.title: Who's Afraid of Virginia Woolf?
picks.note: Mike Nichols adapting Edward Albee, with Elizabeth Taylor and Richard Burton as George and Martha tearing each other apart over one night.

picks.title: Glengarry Glen Ross
picks.note: David Mamet's real estate salesmen, directed by James Foley. 'Always be closing.'

picks.title: A Few Good Men
picks.note: Aaron Sorkin's own play, adapted for the screen, and a perennial pick on Big Picture top fives.

segments.heading: Top 10 Stage-to-Screen: The Musicals
segments.note: five picks each, stage musicals only, no Hollywood-original musicals like Singin' in the Rain

picks.title: Brigadoon
picks.note: Vincente Minnelli's musical about a hidden Scottish village that appears once every hundred years; the movie Francis Ford Coppola told Ryan Coogler to watch before making Black Panther. Not Sean's number one.

picks.title: Bye Bye Birdie
picks.note: A last-minute add for Amanda, led by Ann-Margret's opening number, which was written for the film version rather than the stage show.

picks.title: Cabaret
picks.note: Bob Fosse's Weimar-set follow-up to the flop of his first movie musical, Sweet Charity, with Liza Minnelli. Won Best Picture.

picks.title: My Fair Lady
picks.note: George Cukor directing Audrey Hepburn (dubbed by Marni Nixon) in the Lerner and Loewe musical. Julie Andrews originated the role onstage but lost the film casting, then won her Oscar the same year for Mary Poppins instead.

picks.title: The Sound of Music
picks.note: Robert Wise directing Julie Andrews and Christopher Plummer in the Rodgers and Hammerstein musical, shot on location in Austria. A childhood-formative, annual-rewatch pick for Amanda.

picks.title: Little Shop of Horrors
picks.note: Frank Oz directing the Alan Menken/Howard Ashman musical (itself based on Roger Corman's low-budget original), with Rick Moranis and Ellen Greene.

picks.title: Topsy-Turvy
picks.note: Mike Leigh's backstage drama about Gilbert and Sullivan staging The Mikado, ending with an extended performance of the operetta itself.

picks.title: Grease
picks.note: A peak childhood-sleepover pick, spun off into Grease 2 and later live TV versions.

picks.title: West Side Story
picks.note: Both hosts' shared number one. Directed by Robert Wise and Jerome Robbins, from the Bernstein/Sondheim/Arthur Laurents stage musical, with Rita Moreno's performance and Robbins's choreography singled out. Steven Spielberg's remake was due out for its 60th anniversary.

segments.heading: In Conversation
segments.note: on playing Malcolm X in One Night in Miami and Barack Obama in The Comey Rule

films.title: 21 Grams
films.note: Ben-Adir's answer to the show's closing question, 'the last great thing you've seen': rewatched the night before, on an Alejandro G. Iñárritu kick.

films.title: Babel
films.note: Also watched that same morning as part of his Iñárritu binge, though 21 Grams was his official pick.
